text: The video demonstrates the construction of an ileorectal anastomosis using a single-port laparoscopic technique. The surgical field shows an end ileostomy and a rectal stump, remnants from a previous total colectomy performed for Crohn's disease.

The surgeon fully mobilizes the end ileostomy and places the anvil of a circular stapler into its lumen. The distal ileum is returned into the peritoneum, and an Alexis wound protector is positioned at the stoma site. A glove is placed on the outer ring of the wound protector, with laparoscopic trocars inserted into its fingers, converting the stoma site into a single-port portal.

The laparoscopic view through the surgical glove port at the site of the mobilized end ileostomy reveals the top of the distal colorectal stump buried subfascially. The distal sigmoid and its mesocolon remain in situ, contributing to a straightforward procedure with minimal interperitoneal adhesions.

The surgeon uses a 30-degree video scope for enhanced visualization while mobilizing the top of the colorectal stump. A ligature device is employed alongside downward traction with a retracting instrument to efficiently mobilize the stump within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon performs cross-stapling on the colorectal stump to create a fresh, well-vascularized end for the ileorectal anastomosis. The rectal stump is visible intraperitoneally, indicating that it may have been shortened during the initial operation.

The surgeon uses a sixty millimeter laparoscopic stapler to fully transect the colorectal stump. The stapler is inserted into one of the glove port fingers and tied off after removal to avoid using an additional trocar. The transected cuff of the colorectum is withdrawn into an unused finger of the glove port, which is then clamped off with the specimen inside, maintaining pneumoperitoneum.

The surgeon assesses the ileum for sufficient length to reach the top of the distal colorectal stump for a tension-free anastomosis. Upon finding inadequate length, the surgeon performs further dissection to divide interloop adhesions between the small bowel and its mesentery, restoring mobility to the small bowel.

The surgeon passes the head of the circular stapler through the anus, positioning its spike just posterior to the cross staple line. The stapler head is then aligned with the spike of the anvil stapler, allowing for the construction of the anastomosis under direct endoscopic visualization.

The surgeon uses a sigmoidoscope to test the integrity of the submerged anastomosis by descending it with air, checking for any leakage of air bubbles. Following the successful leak test, the stoma site incision is closed, marking the conclusion of the operation.
